site.title: Imbabura Hydroclimatic Geoportal
site.description: Bilingual scientific geoportal with multi-source hydroclimatic evidence for Imbabura Province, Ecuador, linked to the manuscript submitted to the International Journal of Climatology.

home.hero_subtitle: A scientific-territorial showcase of observed, satellite, reanalysed and projected climate evidence for the province — linked to the manuscript submitted to the International Journal of Climatology.
home.what_obs_desc: 1991–2020 climatology over 21 INAMHI stations with CHIRPS, ERA5-Land and TerraClimate products.
home.what_trends_desc: Mann–Kendall + Theil–Sen tests with MMK Hamed-Rao sensitivity and FDR-BH correction across 7 variables.
home.what_enso_desc: Spearman correlation between monthly ONI and local hydroclimatic series, 588 tests with 0–3 month lags.
home.what_cmip6_desc: BASD-PE 10-GCM ensemble under SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5 to the 2041–2070 horizon.
home.context_p1: Imbabura Province, in the tropical Andes of northern Ecuador, occupies 4,599 km² between 0.12° and 0.88° N and between 77.81° and 79.28° W, with elevations ranging from 675 m at the Chontal depression to 4,939 m at the Cotacachi-Cayapas volcanic complex — an altitudinal range of 2,465 m within less than 100 km.
home.context_p2: Three hydrographic units converge here: the Mira River basin (Pacific slope), the endorheic San Pablo lake microbasin, and the headwaters of the Intag River (a tributary of the Guayllabamba). This topographic heterogeneity allows quantification of differentiated climatic responses within a bounded territory.
home.context_p3: The provincial INAMHI network covers 21 stations (18 Imbabura + 3 Carchi) with digitised 1994–2013 coverage and two automatic stations operating 2014–2025 (M0105 Otavalo and M1240 Ibarra-INAMHI).
home.why_title: Why does climate research in Imbabura matter?
home.why_p1: Tropical mountain regions concentrate strong climatic gradients within short distances, but rarely provide long, homogeneous observational series. With its extreme altitudinal gradient and direct exposure to eastern tropical Pacific variability, Imbabura offers a natural laboratory for assessing climate change signals in data-sparse Andean territories.
home.why_p2: Documenting climate change in these systems is critical for páramo ecosystems, mountain water balance, and communities depending on Andean agriculture and water resources.
home.explore_subtitle: Start with the scientific viewer, where the research is read across six sequential modes.

viewer.title: Scientific climate viewer
viewer.subtitle: Six modes: Diagnostic, Trends, Validation, ENSO, CMIP6 projections, Traceability.

A.label: Diagnostic
A.question: What climate does Imbabura have?
A.intro: This mode shows the observational network, 1991–2020 climatology, and the altitudinal bands that structure provincial climatic heterogeneity.
A.interp: Imbabura presents a strong altitudinal gradient that conditions the spatial distribution of temperature, precipitation and observed climatic response.

B.label: Trends
B.question: Where do climate variables increase or decrease?
B.intro: Mann–Kendall + Theil–Sen slopes for 7 variables, with MMK Hamed-Rao sensitivity and FDR-BH correction at q=0.05.
B.interp: Significant generalised warming in 14/16 independent ERA5 pixels, and a precipitation-increase cluster on the western slope (M0086, M0103, M0104, M0314).

C.label: Validation
C.question: How reliable are CHIRPS and ERA5-Land?
C.intro: Cross-validation against 2 INAMHI automatic stations (M0105 and M1240) with Pearson r, RMSE, MAE and absolute/relative bias metrics.
C.interp: CHIRPS-M1240 is acceptable for precipitation; ERA5-Land shows a systematic warm bias of +8.7 °C over the inter-Andean valley — valid for temporal trends but not for absolute point magnitudes.

D.label: ENSO
D.question: Which variables respond most to El Niño?
D.intro: Spearman correlation between monthly ONI and local climate series with 0–3 month lags; FDR-BH correction over 588 tests.
D.interp: Significant negative precip-ONI correlation at all 21 stations (median ρ=−0.23, lag-3 dominant); 463 of 588 tests retained after FDR-BH.

E.label: CMIP6
E.question: What might happen under different scenarios?
E.intro: BASD-CMIP6-PE 10-GCM ensemble under SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5 with median change and inter-model IQR by altitudinal band.
E.interp: Projected warming of +1.1 to +2.1 °C by 2041–2070 with 100 % sign agreement and modest altitudinal amplification (EDW) of ~0.25 °C between B3 and B1.
E.warning: Projections represent regional and altitudinal-band climate signals; they should not be interpreted as point predictions for a specific station or parish (model scale ~100 km).

F.label: Traceability
F.question: Where does each number come from?
F.intro: Navigable Table S10: every numerical claim in the manuscript linked to its raw input, intermediate file, production script and final output.
F.interp: End-to-end reproducibility with Python scripts and documented seeds; Zenodo concept DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19821757.

validation_status.LIMITED_desc: Use with caution; report uncertainty (0.40 ≤ r < 0.60 or elevated bias).
validation_status.REJECTED_FOR_ABSOLUTE_desc: Not for absolute point magnitudes; valid for temporal trends (systematic bias cancels in Δt).

results.title: Results
results.subtitle: Scientific synthesis of manuscript findings (sections 4.1 to 4.5).
results.s41_p: ERA5-Land shows a coherent regional warming signal: 14 of 16 independent pixels (88 %) present significant positive Theil-Sen slopes, median +0.0112 °C/yr (~+0.49 °C in 44 years). Per-station equivalents: 19/21 significant and +0.52 °C in 44 years, with maxima of +0.71 to +0.74 °C at inter-Andean valley and páramo locations (M0104, M0909, M0001, M0317, M0328).
results.s42_p: ETCCDI indices on daily CHIRPS show significant intensification without volumetric change: Rx1day +0.49 mm/yr; Rx5day +0.71 mm/yr; R95p +4.17 mm/yr. CDD increases in western basins (+0.21 days/yr). Result consistent with the Clausius-Clapeyron thermodynamic hypothesis.
results.s43_p: Significant negative Spearman correlation between precipitation and ONI at all 21 stations (median ρ=−0.23, 3-month dominant lag at 17 stations). After global FDR-BH on 588 tests, 463 (78.7 %) remain significant, confirming robustness to multiple-testing.
results.s44_p: The BASD-CMIP6-PE ensemble reproduces the warming direction with 100 % sign agreement among the 10 GCMs. The CMIP6 median Sen slope (+0.021 to +0.024 °C/yr) is approximately twice the ERA5-Land observational value (+0.0118 °C/yr), consistent with reports for other mountain regions.
results.s45_p: Projected warming of +1.09 °C (SSP1-2.6) to +2.11 °C (SSP5-8.5) by the 2041–2070 horizon, with modest altitudinal amplification (+0.15 °C between B1 and B3 under SSP5-8.5). Precipitation +5 to +8 % and ET₀-HS +3 to +7 %, configuring a future regime of greater seasonal hydroclimatic stress.

stations.title: Stations & validation
stations.subtitle: Complete catalogue of the 21 INAMHI stations (18 Imbabura + 3 Carchi) with metadata, location, altitudinal band, ERA5-Land pixel assignment and cross-validation metrics.

methodology.title: Methodology
methodology.subtitle: Reproducible pipeline, data sources, quality control, statistical methods and limitations.
methodology.lim_1: ERA5-Land resolution (~9 km) generates pixel collision: 5 pixels host 2 stations each (10/21 stations).
methodology.lim_2: TerraClimate PET trend loses significance under MMK Hamed-Rao due to high lag-1 autocorrelation (0.47).
methodology.lim_3: MOD16A2GF covers only 2001–2024; trends do not survive FDR-BH and are reported only as directional consistency.
methodology.lim_4: CMIP6 ensemble is already bias-adjusted; observation contrast is in sign and relative magnitude, not closed validation.

data.title: Open data
data.subtitle: Direct download of the 13 geoportal layers and link to the Zenodo deposit with persistent DOI.
data.zenodo_card_desc: Original CSV tables, Python scripts, high-resolution figures, metadata and permanent archive of the IJC manuscript.
data.tables_xlsx_desc: 11 sheets: T1 to T7 (main manuscript) + S7 to S10 (Supporting Information).
data.manuscript_desc: Version submitted to the International Journal of Climatology (Wiley · 27 April 2026).
data.supp_desc: 12 supplementary figures S1–S12, 10 tables S1–S10 and Appendix A.

traceability.title: Scientific traceability
traceability.subtitle: Table S10 — every numerical claim in the manuscript linked to its raw input, intermediate file, production script and final output.

gallery.title: Scientific & territorial gallery
gallery.subtitle: 22 figures from the Wiley submission package (10 main + 12 supplementary) organised by manuscript section.

about.title: About the project
about.subtitle: Author, academic citation, licence and contact.
about.author_role: Independent researcher · Specialist in geoinformatics and applied climatology
about.cite_apa: Pinto-Páez, V. H. (2026). Hydroclimatic trends, ENSO teleconnection, and CMIP6 projections in Imbabura Province, Ecuador: a traceable regional hydroclimatic baseline with projected elevation-dependent warming, 1981–2070. International Journal of Climatology [under review]. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19821757
